pattern: Converting a calendar date to an ordinal day reduces to summing the lengths of the months that precede it, with a leap-year correction for February.
hints: The answer is the day-of-month plus the total number of days in every month that comes before it.
Hard-code the day counts for the twelve months; the only variable is whether February has 28 or 29 days.
A year is a leap year when it is divisible by 4 but not by 100, unless it is also divisible by 400; add one to February's length in that case.

edge_inputs.description: last day of a non-leap year
after February in a leap year shifts the count by one
1900 is divisible by 100 but not 400, so not a leap year

stress.description: random valid Gregorian dates in the allowed window
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    y = rng.randint(1900, 2019)
    leap = (y % 4 == 0 and y % 100 != 0) or (y % 400 == 0)
    dim = [31, 29 if leap else 28, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31]
    m = rng.randint(1, 12)
    d = rng.randint(1, dim[m - 1])
    return ['%04d-%02d-%02d' % (y, m, d)]
stress.note: always constructs a real date so the day count is well-defined

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def dayOfYear(self, date):
        y, m, d = int(date[:4]), int(date[5:7]), int(date[8:10])
        leap = (y % 4 == 0 and y % 100 != 0) or (y % 400 == 0)
        count = 0
        cur_m, cur_d = 1, 1
        while (cur_m, cur_d) != (m, d):
            count += 1
            dim = [31, 29 if leap else 28, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31][cur_m - 1]
            cur_d += 1
            if cur_d > dim:
                cur_d = 1
                cur_m += 1
        return count + 1
